title: NOC 94131 – Weavers, knitters and other fabric making occupations | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s textile industry is undergoing a high-tech transformation. Weavers, Knitters, and Fabric Making Machine Operators (NOC 94131) are no longer just manual laborers; they are technical experts operating sophisticated, computer-controlled looms and knitting systems. These machines produce everything from high-performance industrial fabrics and medical-grade mesh to sustainable textiles for the fashion industry. With a significant portion of the current workforce approaching retirement, there is a steady need for international workers who can bridge the gap between traditional craftsmanship and modern automated production.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

duties: Machine Setup: Setting up looms or knitting machines according to complex digital or physical patterns.
Operational Oversight: Operating batteries of machines to produce fabric, hosiery, garments, or industrial products like nets and twine.
Pattern Preparation: Reading loom patterns and preparing mechanical or digital pattern mechanisms.
Quality Monitoring: Inspecting fabrics for defects (missed stitches, tension issues) and performing minor mechanical adjustments.
Maintenance: Cleaning and lubricating machines and replacing needles or worn components.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Training: Several months of on-the-job training are standard, with duration depending on the complexity of the machinery.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer in the same company is often preferred for operator roles.
Physical Demands: Good hand-eye coordination, manual dexterity, and the ability to stand for long shifts in industrial environments.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) indicates a "Balanced" market. While automation improves efficiency, the need for skilled operators to manage complex patterns and high-performance materials remains high. With nearly 50% of the workforce expected to retire over the next decade, international recruitment is vital to sustain Canada's textile manufacturing capacity.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by stability in traditional hubs and growth in technical textile sectors:
Quebec (Montreal & Eastern Townships): Strong demand. Quebec remains Canada’s textile heartland, with over 60% of jobs in this sector.
Ontario (GTA): Stable demand. Focused on industrial fabrics, automotive textiles, and mattress manufacturing.
British Columbia: Moderate prospects. Driven by specialized outdoor apparel and marine-grade textile production in the Lower Mainland.
Nova Scotia: Stable prospects. Small but consistent needs in specialized weaving and rope-making facilities.
Why Work in Canada as a Weaver or Knitter?
Modern Technical Environment: Shift from manual looms to operating automated, multi-feed knitting machines and digital jacquard looms.
Diverse Industry Applications: Work in sectors ranging from fashion and home decor to aerospace and healthcare (technical textiles).
Path to PR: Frequently eligible for Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) semi-skilled streams and the 2026 TR to PR transition streams.
Skills Evolution: Opportunity to learn digital pattern reproduction and machine maintenance, which are highly valued in the 2026 manufacturing landscape.
pathways: 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated stream for temporary workers with 12 months of Canadian experience. Textile operators are prioritized under "Essential Manufacturing."
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer.
Quebec Regular Skilled Worker Program (RSWP): An excellent route for those working in Montreal's vibrant textile sector.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for semi-skilled manufacturing workers to obtain Permanent Residency.
